ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002572); no prawn-specific biryani subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers biryani as a rice dish category; no prawn-specific facet applied. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for prawn biryani or biryani as a food product. None assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of prawn biryani is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of spiced basmati rice layered with whole prawns, caramelised onions, and aromatic garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Biryani (Prawn) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central (Shrimp, cooked, SR Legacy 15152) for prawn component cross-check
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides composite biryani values. Prawn biryani is estimated by adapting the ICMR-NIN chicken biryani baseline (energy ~170 kcal/100g) and substituting the protein and fat profile with cooked prawn data from USDA SR Legacy (15152). The resulting composite reflects a typical prawn biryani at approximately 60% rice and 40% prawn-masala component by weight. Values are cross-checked against published Indian dietary survey data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice-based mixed dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for prawn biryani. GI estimated at 50 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice (GI ~50–58, mean ~52) and the diluting effect of the high-protein prawn component and fat from ghee, which slow gastric emptying and reduce the glycaemic response. The composite dish GI is conservatively estimated at 50 (low category, ≤55). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (50 × 75.25) / 100 = 37.6 g (serving size 350 g, carbohydrate 21.5 g/100g → 75.25 g per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100g; ~1470 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit prawn biryani to small portions (≤150 g) and request reduced salt preparation, as a standard restaurant serving can contribute approximately 60–70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~37.6 per 350 g serving) despite low GI basmati rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume prawn biryani in controlled portions (≤150–200 g) to limit the glycaemic load per meal; the high-protein prawn content and fat from ghee help moderate the postprandial glucose response compared with plain white rice dishes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol from prawns (~95 mg/100g) and saturated fat from ghee (~1.8 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit prawn biryani to occasional consumption and request ghee-reduced preparation, as the combined cholesterol and saturated fat load from a full restaurant serving may contribute meaningfully to daily lipid intake targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Biryani (also known as Jheenga Biryani in Hindi) is a fragrant, layered South Asian rice dish in which whole or halved prawns are marinated in yoghurt and spices, then slow-cooked together with long-grain basmati rice using the traditional dum method — a sealed-pot technique that traps steam and allows the rice and seafood to cook in their own aromatic juices. The dish is characterised by its golden saffron-tinted rice, caramelised onion topping, and the interplay of whole spices such as cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaf with fresh mint and coriander. The texture contrasts fluffy, separate grains of rice with succulent, slightly firm prawns. Prawn biryani is a popular menu item in Indian and Indian-diaspora restaurants across Singapore, where it is commonly served with raita (spiced yoghurt) and a boiled egg. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~168 kcal/100g), a meaningful protein contribution from prawns (~9.2 g/100g), and a moderate carbohydrate load from basmati rice (~21.5 g/100g). The GI is estimated at 50 (low category) due to the basmati rice base and the protein-fat matrix from prawns and ghee. Sodium content is elevated (~420 mg/100g) due to spice blends and cooking salt, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Prawns are a source of vitamin B12, selenium, and cholesterol, which are clinically relevant for cardiovascular risk assessment.
common_user_queries: How many calories in prawn biryani?
Is prawn biryani high in sodium?
What is the GI of prawn biryani?
What spices are in prawn biryani?

confidence_reason: No exact prawn biryani entry was found in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken biryani baseline with prawn substitution cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp data. GI is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is defensible and the component sources are approved primary sources.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN biryani composite or the USDA shrimp SR Legacy entry for this dish context. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary sources used for this adapted record. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from the ghee and prawn fat profile using USDA SR Legacy component data; these are -1 sentinel values replaced with estimated numerics where defensible component data exists. The food_name has been normalised from the input 'jheenga Biryani' to the canonical English form 'Prawn Biryani'; the Hindi romanization 'Jheenga Biryani' is retained as a primary alias. This dish is served at Kinara - Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 22.00. The cuisine is classified as Indian (North Indian / Mughlai tradition). Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (Indian Muslim cuisine); kosher status is not applicable. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (50) × carbohydrate per serving in grams (21.5 g/100g × 350 g = 75.25 g) / 100 = 37.6.
